notes: Digital introduced LinkWorks in 1994; on third major release by April 1997
LinkWorks in wide use by many Fortune 1000 firms
WebWorker enables any user with a web browser to participate in LinkWorks environment
Aberdeen research indicates less than 50% of deployed browsers support Java in 1997
Server supported on Digital UNIX, HP-UX, OpenVMS, IBM AIX, Windows NT 3.51 and 4.0
Desktop support includes MS Windows 95, NT client, Motif variants, Mac OS, WebWorker browsers
Supports Oracle, Microsoft SQL Server, CA-Ingres, or Informix as repository databases
Digital has been delivering distributed object product for 3+ years vs competitors who only have white papers
LinkWorks integrates to Windows desktop using OLE automation component of COM
Digital found deploying prototype demands another level of effort vs single-instance prototype
Digital working to become BackOffice Certified for seamless Microsoft environment integration
Companies with direct customer interaction find extroverted applications consistently higher ROI
Sun, Netscape, IBM, Microsoft all written white papers proclaiming benefits of distributed object framework
Netscape future browser with native Object Request Broker expected in 6-12 months from April 1997
Digital can adopt Java, create Java app delivering LinkWorks to any platform, or integrate Netscape ORB
Major vendors all positioning distributed objects as technology to tame the internet
IBM adopting JavaBeans as local object components and will support CORBA for interoperability
LinkWorks will be able to leverage DCOM as Microsoft slowly delivers it
DEC acquired by Compaq for $9.6B in June 1998; LinkWorks product line fate uncertain
CORBA/distributed objects largely replaced by SOAP web services by 2003 and REST by 2007
COM/DCOM/ActiveX evolved into .NET component model; ActiveX deprecated in modern browsers post-2015
Java became one of world's most widely used enterprise languages; JavaBeans less dominant but Java EE active
Netscape Navigator discontinued January 2008; Mozilla Firefox (spun off from Netscape) became dominant open-source browser
Digital UNIX renamed Tru64 UNIX; HP ended support in 2012
Distributed object framework enables rapid application development significantly reducing TCO
Once legacy apps integrated into LinkWorks they can be tied into any number of LinkWorks solutions
Legacy programmers can be retrained as object-oriented experts using LinkWorks
Aberdeen urges companies to evaluate LinkWorks; at minimum learn why every vendor rushing to distributed objects
